title: Lesson 402
transcript: Service accounts. Service accounts are accounts that are used by something other than a human. So service accounts are used by systems, batch jobs, things like that. Think if there's a web server that needs to access a backend database. that web server should have its own service account into the database server. Or if there's a batch job that needs to run and store some data on an FTP server, that batch job should probably have a service account on that FTP server. So service accounts are simply accounts that are used by applications, systems, or something like that, specifically not by humans. And service account management is obviously managing these service accounts, managing the creation process. So when are these accounts required to be used and making sure that, you know, if there's a web server accessing a backend database, What are the absolute minimum number of permissions that the service account needs to allow the web server to query the database? you know, to gather the information that needs, but nothing more. Like should the service account have the ability to delete tables, drop the entire database? Probably not. The service account should probably just be allowed to update or read records, maybe delete records, things like that. So as part of the creation process, you want to make sure that service accounts only have the permissions they need. And as part of overall account management, you want to make sure that you have an inventory of all of these accounts that, as I mentioned, the permissions are appropriate, minimum needed. that there's some process in place to periodically review these accounts and review the permissions. There's a process in place for emergencies. What if a system is compromised or a service account is compromised? How can you go about changing that service account credential? All of this falls under service account management, maintaining these service accounts.
